Objective: Determine whether one or more components in the
cigarette blend make disproportionately large contributions to the
total amount of dust generated at the Primary in Cabarrus.
Results: Using control blends, multiple linear regression analysis
revealed that an accurate mathematical model for predicting the
obaceo composition of these dust samples required only four
Predictor (or independent} variables: calcium, aspartic acid, B-
methylvaleric acid {ratio of sample te standard) and cellulose
index. "4

Sonclusions: “It was found that there was a significant, but highly
variable, content of sand in every dust sample. Comparison of the
Bredicted compositions of the dust samples with the ratios of the
cigarette blend components actually processed at the Cabarrus
Prinazy led to a number of findings: (1) there 1s a relationship
between collection site and composition of dust sample, (2)
Oriental leaf appears to be the most friable of all the tobacco
components, (3) bright lamina and burley lamina are the least
friable tobacco components, and (4) stem, which is a major
component of RL, has intermediate friability.

In summary, there were two major conclusions; (1) Oriental leaf is
the only tobacco companent which hag a disproportionately great
tendency to form dust and (2) sand is prevalent in all the dust.
samples. s

Blans: Analyses also will be run on ancther set of dust samples
that has been requested from the Cabarrus Primary. In
collaboration with Mary Ellen Counts, @ friability study will pe
run on DBC bright, DBC burley and MT uncased filler to see if
supporting data can be obtained regarding the tendency of Oriental
leaf te form dust. In addition, silicen analyses will be performed
on a number of tobacce components in an effort to determine the
Source of the sand in the Primary.

PORTABLE AIR SAMPLING SYSTEM. «PASS)
RESEARCH METHODS

In measuring ETS, equipment and procedures originally developed by the
Harvard School of Public Health were used. Samples were collected unobtrusively
with battery-operated, portable air sample systems appearing from the outside to
be ordinary briefcases. This unobtrusive approach made it possible noi to
disturb the ordinary behavior of office workers and diners. On the outside,
each bricfcase has an on-off switch concealed beneath the handle and several
brass openings through which air samples enter.

‘The inside of each briefesse contains a computer and devices for collecting
nicotine ~~ the major indicator of tobacco smoke in the air. The computer gives
information regarding the amount of air sampled. Nicotine in the air is
collected with « small glass tube containing a special absorbing material, The
tube is placed through one of the briefcase’s brass openings. A pump is used 10 «
draw the air sample into the glass tube.

The process of sampling particles used another brass opening. The process
may be viewed as happening in two steps, First, air is dram by a pomp through
a device that separates small particles, which include tobacco smoke, from
larger particles in the air. The small particles are then collected on a
filter.

Samples collected during the study were shipped 10 teboratories at
Toternational Technology Corporation where they were analyzed. For analyzing
nicotine, a more sophisticated version of the method employed by the U.S.
National Institute of Occupational Safety and Health was used.

1. ABSTRACT
Regulatory bodies in the United States and Europe have recently used a group of

epidemiology studies on exposure to environmental tobacco smoke (ETS) and

development of cardiovascular disease (CVD) to conclude that ETS causes tens of

thousands of CVD deaths per year. This conclusion i

ited by at least two major
diagnostic flaws in the premises upon which the epidemiology studies are based. First,
causes of death listed on death certificates are used as surrogates for mortality statistics. A.
comprehensive review of the literature comparing the accuracy of death certificate data
‘with autopsy and medical record-validated causes of death demonstrates a large error rate
‘on the death certificates. This large death certificate error rate for CVD is propagated,
without critique, throughout the calculation of a very small increase in relative tisk
associated with BTS. Second, these epidemiotogy stadies ignore a diagnostic bias against
smokers and their spouses. On a comparative basis, the diagnostic bias tends to
underestimate the incidence of CVD during the lifetime of the smoker and his/her spouse,
and conversely, to overestimate the incidence of CVD specifically, the frequent (default
diagnosis of myocardial infarction) at death in this group. A major influence underlying
this diagnostic bias is the relative lack of information concerning CVD in smokers and
their spouses (as compared to nonsmokers). Socioeconomic, geographic and
psychological factors in smoking households lead to inferior health insurance coverage,

reduction in diagnostic and therapeutic procedures, relative lack of medical records, poor

STATEMENT ON AMMONIA COMPOUNDS.
Philip Morris U.S.A.

Facts about Ammonia and Cigarettes

Ammonium (NH,*) salts are natural components of tobacco. Several
ammonia (NHs) related compounds ere used by Philip Morris in the
Cigarette manufacturing process. Relevant to this inquiry, the compounds
in current use by Philip Morris include: Diammonium Phosphate (DAP)
listed as Ammonium Phosphate Dibasic on the ingredients list), Urea,
Ammonium Hydroxide, and Ammonium Bicarbonate. These substances
have been identified to the Department of Health and Human Services
since 1986 as a part of the U.S. tobacco Industry ingredients ist. In
addition, six independent scientists have reviewed the usage level of
these substances in the manufacture of cigarettes and have concluded
they are not harmful to consumers.

Jn addition to their use in cigarette manufacturing, these ammonia
related compounds are.used in many other industries and are commonly
found in a variety of consumer products. Diammenium Phosphate is
used in alcoholic beverages, ice cream, ices, gelatin and puddings
Urea is a natural component of human metabolism, tobacco, and cats
and is also found in alcoholic beverages, bakery products and gelatin,
Ammonium Hydroxide is purchased as an arnmonie solution in water
and is significantly diluted prior to use. Ammonia is a naturally occurring
substance that plays a vital role in protein metabolism. Ammonium
Bicarbonate is used in food, baking powder formulations, cocoa
products, and baked goods. All four ingredients are on the FDA list of
substances generally recognized as safe (GRAS) for use in foods.

Ammonia felated compounds are used as processing aids and flavorants
in the manufacture of cigarettes. Processing aids ate added to tobacco
in the manufacturing procass and do not substantively persist in the
finished product. Flavorants are added to tobacco to enhance the
sensory acceptability of the product. As a flavorant in cigarettes, the
soluble ammonia aval in tobacco appears to correlate with the. sensory
“impact' of the smoke. Impact is better described as a feeling sensation
rather than an odor or taste. This impact sensation is comparable to the
effect one feels in the throat from the carbonation of a soft drink, the hot in
chili peppers, or the cool feeling of mint.

Background Of The Invention

The present invention relates to » process for
aking a carbon source and to a smoking article comprising
the carbon source and a flavor generator. More particularly,
the present invention relates to a process for producing a
carbon source from a proformed Ligno-cellulosic material
and te @ smoking article, such as a cigarette, vhich includes
the carbon source and a flaver genexator.

One previously disclosed smoking acticle comprises
a tube formed of combustible material which has a mouthpiece
attached at one end. An axial inner tube of material, which
is breakable when heated, is contained within the tube of
conbustible material and ie coated on ite inner surtace with
an additive material euch as nicotine. Thus, on smoking,
hot gases are drawn through the inner tube and release the
nicotine in the form of an atvesol for inhalation by the
smoker. With this device, hovever, there is an appreciable
loss of nicotine and other desirable conpounds, such as
Mavorants, during smolder. There is also a tendency for

Passive dosimeters for measuring personal exposure ta
hydrogen cyanide and formaldehyde vapors were tested for
their sensitivity, replicability and the ability to dotect
HCN and HCHO originating from passive cigarette smoke. The
lower sensitivity levels for neat HCN and HCHO were 0.2
ppm-hr and 1 ppm-hr, respectively. Whole cigarette smoke
sidestream, containing 45 ppm of added HCN, gives a response
of about 50% of what is predicted from the neat HCN calibra-
tion curve. Similarly, only about 50% of neat HCN added to
whole smoke is registered by the badges. Neither whole
sidestream smoke containing 1.5 ppm of HCHO nor the same
smoke with 50 ppm added HCHO registered on the badges.
Apparently, cigarette smoke aerosol is an efficient scrubber
for (ICN and HCHO making them unavailable for vapor phase
absorption on the badges. Six hour badge exposures to
specially generated smoke-filled room atmospheres and in
ordinary laboratory surroundings failed to register any HCN

ox HCHO.

Program : Synthetic Compounds
Objective:

Determine the biological activity of nicotine analogues in several models suitable to
quantify their peripheral pharmacologicat effects and systemic toxicity.

Start: Apr. 1876
Termination: Nov, 1984
Description of projects:

INBIFO was requested to perform selected quantitative screening assays on 92 nicotine
analogs provided by PMR. The results were reported on a regular basis to be incorporated
by PMR in the overalt program data base. S-()-Nicotine or S.R-(-,+}-nicotine [2] as
bitartrate or free base was used as a reference substance or internal standard.

Altogether 14 assays were used for testing nicotine analogs. Some of the assays were
standard pharmacological assays (0.9. guinea pig ileum}, which were alleady fully
established at INBIFO at the beginning of the program, others had to be established or
refined during its course (e.g. rat vas deferens), and othars proved to be unsuitable (e.g.
frog posture) during the course of the progiam. Therefore most compounds were
investigated in some but not all assays. Another restriction in the application of the assay
battery was the limited amount of test substance available,

Isolated investigations with nicotine as a reference substance (eg. LDS0EDS0
determinations after adminitration by other routes than intravenously or in other mouse
strains) were performed to establish, optimize or belter understand assays and their results,
For the same reason a series of other reference substances were investigated in some of
the screening assays,

the ca ¢t depth has the greatest chance of subjective acceptance. This
condition would require lover drying temperatures than a full 100% increase
in apron speed. A control run Will be made at normal production operating
conditions with both dryers #3 and #4 running. In addition, 250 pounds of
wurley from the dryers will be collected to produce 100% burley cigarettes
at the R&D Semi-works.

OBJECTIVE

Monitor product quality and implement cigarette construction
modifications when necessary, to ensure that the quality
objectives of the product are met.

IMPLEMENTED MODIFICATIONS

During the Ist quarter 1987, some 66 cigarette and some 27 filter
Product specifications were prepared (new or modified) .

The following main modifications were implemented:

The cigarette papers WP 70/110-6 were replaced by WP 60 /137-1,
on PHH’s Marlboro 100s cigarettes MLH01/02, to increase smoke
delivery.

The tipping papers 4M 0.15.4.5, of Ambassador cigarettes MAAO3/08
and HAKO3 produced by PMH, and MAAO4/05 and MAKOS/05, produced by
PIR, were replaced by 34 0.15.4,5 papers, to increase smoke
delivery.

The tipping papers 24/70, on FTR’s Muratti 2000 cigarettes MABO]
and HAHO1, were replaced by 23/60 papers, to increase smoke
delivery,

In January, the tipping paper of PTR's Brunette Double Filtre
BRDOL changed from 24/80 to 23/60, and the special BRD filter was
replaced by the current MAA filter. —

The tipping paper 23/60 of FTR's MLKI5/41 was replaced by a 24/40
paper, to increase smoke delivery.

The filter RTD of PM-UK's Raffles was reduced from 340 to 320 mm
we.

THE INDOOR AIR INDEX (IAD)

A PRACTICAL METHOD TO RANK BUILDING INDOOR AIR POLLUTION.

Brief Proposal by

Demetrios J. Moschandreas, Ph.D.

Many building managers are aware of issues relating to potential indoor air problems, but they
do not spend moneys to investigate and, if necessary, mitigate the problem. They claim, that
present day science measures only snapshots of building conditions and can not measure the
relative magnitude of their problem. Furthermore, they claim that present day science can not
assure them that, after certain expenditures, the problem is corrected because there are no legal
limits or means to put the new conditions in perspective. Consequently, most building decision
makers await for scientific breakthroughs. Research to formulate indoor air quality standards
and to fully characterize indoor air quality, if possible, requires @ very long time. An easier
solution is to formulate a global indoor air index, say from 1 to LO { 10 the most polluted and 1
the least polluted). Like similar environmental indexes, an indoor air index will (1) delineate the
relative magnitude of indoor air pollution of a subject building relative to other buildings
istudied; (2) identify a desirable level/index, and (3) help allocate contro! resources optimally.

The objective of the proposed applied project is to formulate and apply an indoor air quality

index. This will be accomplished with the help of existing, in the literature, information.

TITLE OF PAPER: REFINEMENTS TO THE FIC PROCEDURE FOR THE QUANTITATIVE
DETERMINATECN OF TPM IN CIGARETTE SMOKE

STUOY GROUP:

ABSTRACT TEXT:

A Hightweight and disposable filter holder assembly used in the collection
of total particulate matter in cigarette smoke was developed os a replacement
for the currently used Lucite filter holder. Not only are these new holders
lighter in weight (5 grams versus 27 grams for the Lucite holder) but the
inconvenient and time-consuming steps of washing these holders in an alcohol
bath and drying prior ta subsequent use are also eliminated,

During the development of these holders, it was noted that certain types

ngyp Of materials, including Lucite, were very sensitive to changes in the mofsture
fim content of the environment resulting in highly variable TPH results. These

varfations become most significant for extremely low yield cigarettes where
negative TPM deliveries have been frequently encountered during the analysis.
A negative TPM result indicates a loss of weight of the filter assembly during
the smoking procedure. These negative numbers have been obtained even when
the filter pads were clearly stained by the smoke particulate phase indicating
that something was collected during the smoking process. Therefore, the
apparently negative TPN deliveries must be a result of an artifact in the smoke
particulate matter collection procedure. Experimental data are presented
Showing that the observed artifact {s primarily due to some phenomenon occurring
at the surface of the filter holder during, but extraneous to, the particulate
matter collection process. This phenomenon is shown to be attributable to
the inherent property of certain materials, includiag Lucite, to eastly adsorb
and desorb moisture from the environment,

In order to minimize the observed bias in the TPM results and thus allow
for the callection of more meaningful data, a lightwefght and disposable holder
made out of polypropylene and polystyrene was developed, In addition to the
ebvious advantages of being lightweight and disposable, experimental evidence
was presented showing that a negative bias which becomes significant at low
TeM delivery levels is eliminated through the use of these holders because
Of their inherent insensitivity to moisture fluctuations in the environment.

Experimental Detail:

Benzamidoxime was prepared in the usual manner with one
exception. after all the reagents were introduced into the
reaction flask, the solution was brought to a very gentle
reflux for approximately 24 hours. Previous experience
had shown that a vigorous reflux greatly decreased total
reaction time, but isolation of the pure amidoxine was
tedious and time consuming due to copious quantities of

a tarry residue. The milder conditions afforded good
purity benzamidoxime by simply evaporating the solvent

on the rotary evaporator.

The benzamidoxine (125 g.) was placed in a distilling

flask to which was added 250 ml. of acetic anhydride. The
mixture was -then distilled slowly. The early fractions
contained acetic anhydride and acetic acid. The later

cuts were PMO. Crude yield of PMO = 87.68 (white, crystalline).

the extract was assayed in 36 wells of the ELISA plate. Unlike previous studies, in
both of these experiments the wells at the edges of the plate contributed the highest
variation to the data. For example, when ail of the data was analyzed, the RSDS for
both of these expetiments were 15.44% and 9.57%. If the data from the wells at the
edges of the ELISA plates from these same experiments was eliminated from the
analyses, the RSDs decreased to 8.46% and 5.94% for these same experiments, The
‘mean % nicotine in tobacco for these experiments, calculated without the edge well
data, were 2.60% and 2.69% {as is basis).

deviation (RSD) of the nicotine ELISA procedure, Six replicate Grizzel #2
‘Monitor tobacco extracts were prepared and assayed on three separate days, This

objective

The objective of this study was to compare the composition of un-
diluted mainstream smoke of URSUS-59 to that of URSUS-35 and to
the standard reference cigarette IR4F based on selected chemical
parameters.

Experimental

URSUS mainstream smoke was generated using single-port smoking
machines (a). 1R4F mainstream smoke was generated using a 30-port
INBIFO smoking machine. Both URSUS types wore conditioned at 45 to
50 ¢ relative humidity. URSUS-59 was emoked at 15 J with an 0.8 5
on-time, while URSUS-35 was smoked at 20 J with an 1.05 on-time
Depending on the parameter determined, $ or 20 cigarettes were
smoked per sample, The data reported herein represent the means of
3 to 5 repetitions. Results are expressed as yields per cigarette.

Basic smoke parameters (TPH, nicotine, water, glycerin) as well as
selected aldehydes, nitrosamines, phenols, and hydrocarbons were
determined. The analyses of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons,
vinylpyridine, and nitrogen oxides in the mainstream smoke of
URSUS cigarettes are underway.

Denitrification of SEL (1904)
A. Organic Acids (1}
The development work for the determination of acetic, propionic
and butyric acids in SEL has been completed. A special report has
been issued.

Cooked Flavor (2307)
A, Asparagine Determination (2)

Analytical Method Number E-50 was successfully adapted for
AutoAnalyzer 11 determination of asparagine in cooked flavors.
This procedure will replace the HPLC procedure for asparagine. A
special report has been written and will be issued shortly.

B. Amino Sugars (2)
Workhas begun on development of an amino sugar determination

in cooked flavors. The AutoAnalyzer methodology developed by Davis
for amino sugars in tobacco leaf and filler will be utilized (3).

C, Aldehydes (2)

A cursory examination of several cocked flavor samptes has
shown little difference in the aldehyde content. An AutoAnalyzer
procedure was used but the results were not quantitated. The
procedure will be worked out if differences are indicated.

Gtycyrrhizic Acids in Fitler (2305} (4)
Modification of a MacAndrews & Forbes Company HPLC procedure for

glycyrrhizic acid in tobacco products has been made. This determination

js made in order to estimate the licorice in these products. A& special

report will be issued shortly.

Amino Acids by HPLC (a1] tobacco projects) (4)

Work has begun on evaluation of the two basic approaches to amino
acids determinations: (1) ion exchange separation with post column
derivatization and (2) precolum derivatization with reverse phase

separation.

ENDOTORINS AND WASHED COTTON EXPOSURE IN A MODEL CARDROOM

Stephen A. Olenchock, Ph.O., Judith C, Mull, 4.8.
Edward L, Petsonk, M.D., Robert M. Castellan, M.D.
and James A, Merchant, M.D., Dr. P-H.

Division of Respiratory Disease Studies
Nationa] Institute for Occupational Safety and Health
Morgantown, West Virginia 26504

Gram-negative bacteria and their endotoxins are contaminants of many
occupationally-derived organic dusts. They possess biologically active
Properties which, upon inhalation at the workplace, my elicit detrimental
affects on the worker. The lung serves not only as a portal of entry and
absorbance of endotoxins for systemic alteration of normal functions, but also
as a site of localized tissue and cellular damage following inhalation of gram-
negative bacterial endotoxins.

Cotton dust, in particular, has been shown to contain measutable amounts of

+ grammnegative bacteria and their endotoxins. Correlations between decreases
in puloonary function and numbers of gram-negative bacteria have been reported
in the literature. On the other hand, no correlation between endotoxin content
of bulk cotton samples and decrement in pulmonary function (FEV}) during a nodel
cardroom exposure of naive subjects was found. We therefore studied the air-
borne endotoxin levels of washed and unwashed cotton during carding in a model
eardroom. Personal filters were hung on 17 naive (non-byssinotic) subjects
and breathing zone air samples were taken during the 6-hour exposure to cortoa
carding. Cottons from three distinct growing areas, California (CA), Texas
QO, and Hississippi (HS), were used in this study. The cottons were used with
or without prior washing as a mechaniem for (reuoving endotoxins and thereby)
reducing the pulmonary response of the subjects. In addition, studies were
conducted on the effects of various washing techniques on the level of contamin-
ating endotoxins both in the dust and in che air of the model cardroam.

‘Treatnent of the cottons with vater (66°C, approximately 50:1 vater to fiber be
ratio by weight) resoved considerable amounts of the endotoxins. Dust trapped ©
in the personal filter samples of cotton from GA, TK and HS shoved a reduction in@
endotoxin of 652, 95% and 96% respectively when the washed cotton vas compared - BN
to the unvashed cotton from the sane geographical area. Washing condirions g
affected the endotoxin activity of the airborne dust in that rayon wash line
for water (66°C) at @ racio of 50:1 (water to fiber) was the wose effective, — @
renoving 96% of the endotoxins. Cold water (28°C) at high volume (65:1) vas km
the least effective (852) while the effect of hot water, high volume was 7)
intermediate (89%). Use of » different washing procedure, yool scouring,
reduced the endotoxin level by 87%.

Quantification of the airborne endotoxin levels (ng/m?) resulted in a relatively
good correlation with the subjects’ response (FEV,) after exposure. With each
cotton at similar dust levels, the unvashed sauple had the higher endotoxin
level and the subjects responded with a greater drop in FEV, than with the
respective washed cotton. Changing of the washing conditions had little effect
fon the auount of airborne endotoxin. All were effective, similar amounts were
airborne, and pulmonary responses were similar in all washed categories. _

group (Hathway, 1966) to yleld 2,6-di-tert-buty!-4-hydroxymethy/-phenol {BHT alcohol),

including inhibtion (e.9.. lipoxygenase, cytochromes P450), activation (0.g., protacyclin
synthase), and induction (e.g.. glutathione Wansferase, quinone reductase). is

Evaluation of Transgenic Models to Assess Carcinogenicity

Since its inception, the National Toxicology Progzam (NTP) has developed
and evaluated new methods that could be used to predict or identify chemicals or
physical agents which may post a catcinogenic hazard to humans. Information
that has proven to be most predictive including chemical structure, Salmonella
mutagenicity, in vio micronucleus induction, etz., are utilized in the processes
of selecting chemicals for study and in study design. The most recent progress in
this area is the identification of selected transgenic mouse models that appent to
have great promise in identifying potential carcinogens more quickly and at 9
fraction of the cost of the standard rodent cancer bioassay,

Why Focus on Transgenic Models?

Although the transgenic models under study diffe: in the way they were
developed (pronuclear injection of regulated genes or inactivation of genes via
homologous recombinatior)} they were selected for evaluation as carcinogenicity
bicassays because they all have the property of carrying a spacific target gone that
is known to be involved in neoplastic processes bath in humans and rodents.
Within the concept of the mult'-stage or multi-genic pathways of carcinogenesis,
these models are therefore “genetically initiated” an while the specific change
does not itself result in tumor induction within the time limits of the assays, the
animals are preferentially susceptible to the action of carcinogens. The
advantages provided by such an approach are that in contrast to it vitro systems,
chemicals undergo metabolism, distribution and relevant pharmacokinetics ay
they occur in other rodent lines, and the neoplastic effects of agents can be
observed in these models within a timeframe in which few if any spontaneous
tumors arise. The high incidences of spontaneous or background tumors that
‘occur most often late in 2-year rodent cancer studies are among the most
confounding factors in interpreting the findings of chemical carcinogenesis,
studies and determining theic implications for human health. There is alsa
some uncertainty surrounding the significance of neoplastic responses that oveur
in a single species or a single-sex-species as being potentially strain ot rodent-
species specific. It is proposed that these problems can be reduced through the
use of transgenic models since likely genetic targets for the effect of the chemicals
are highly conserved oncogenes or tumor suppressor genes as opposed to the
potentially many unidentified genes that contribute to she carcinogenic response
in conventional rodent lines, The use of such target or reporter genes also
allows for direct molecular and celtular analysis of the chemical effects in these
models providing added information on the mechanism by which the chemicals
act.

Summary 3/16/88

Flow-pressure drop determinations on the front end of the assembled
article, the carbon alone, and the front end with the star in the carbon
plugged have shown that only one third of the flew goes through the star.
These measurements were also made as a function of increasing temperature
and the ratio of flow remaine the same although the flow overall is reduced
because of the increased temperature. From this determination and the
recognition that improvement in heat transfer occurs in the presence of the
star a atrong case can be made for the importance of radiative heat transfer
with the star configuration. In the current design this mode becomes
increasingly important as the effective radiation source (within the ster)
progresses down the star during puffing. It seems that this mode of heat
transfer can possibly be optimized further through absorptivity/emissivity
considerations as well as from a view factor standpoint, These concepts are
being explored in the lab. °

A determination of the oxygen requirements for static smoldering of
Stackpole 7743 carbon inside cardboard of different diameters is being made.
‘the static boundary layer thickness has been estimated to be approximately
Snm for the dimensional and thermal constraints in the current article.
Mass burn rates of carbons are being determined inside impermeable
cardboard cylinders that have different inside wall diemeters. With a 0.5mm
gap between the carbon and the cylinder wall a flow of ~30cc/min is required
to maintain smolder for complete consumption of the ¢lip-supported carbon.

‘The oxygen content of air exiting the cylinder during this combustion has
been reduced from 214 te 54.

1. EXTRUDED TOBACCO

The Wenger cooking extruder was recefved and {s currently being
installed.

Work has been conducted to enhance the overall quality of the
extruded tobacco sheet. Finer particle size (less than 100 mesh)
ROB feedstock appears to improve sheet uniformity and strength.

Further attempts have been made to reduce the quantity of
Klucel in the tobacco formulation. Sheets produced with 5% solids
(58 Klucel, 1o% starch, 85% RCB feedstock} were sat{sfactory. The
presence of starch in the formulation appears to alter the way in
which Klucel fs distributed fn the tobacco.

Inf{tial work was begun with the Haake viscometer to study the
rheology of Klucel solutions at various shear rates. Initial results
Suggest that solutions behave as a pseudoplastic material. Test
results will be used to afd in die design and to establish initial
extrusfon condtttons.

I, ADHESIVE APPLICATION

The Findley adhesive foamar was used to provide SC¢ foamed
adhes{ve for evaluation on the PAs. Test results indicated large
bubble-size formation. Examination of the foamer led ta seyeral
modifications and repairs to generate the correct foam consistency.
Testing is hetng performed to establish taste and odor acceptance
and actual adhesive usage.

At. CAM LUT

Various filter samples have been produced for Manufacturing
Engineering, Samples have been used ta test equipment and evaluate
various filter designs.

Several encapsulated flavors have been obtained from suppliers
to study flavor release potential. Efforts have been directed at
determining which encapsulating materiat (sucrose, malto-dextrin,

etc.) is most susceptible to the moisture fn the fflter during smoking.

Testing has shown that sufficient water 1s present to dissolve the
encapsulating material.

BACKGROUND AND PURPOSE

Three new models have been used for tha Kent “Locker xoom”
campaign. Two of them = Holahan (Golf) and Bailey (Tennis) ~
are being run in March/April. The third, Turner {Tennia), will
run in the Pall.

This Portfolio Test was conducted to determine comparability
and consistency with the Kent Locker Room campaign in terms of
noting and imagery. Specifically, the stopping power,
persuasive ability and communicative content of each of the
ads.

METHOD

This three cell Portfolio Test was conducted among random
non-menthol smokers (roughly 300 per cell) 18 years and older.
The sample was half male and half female. Mall intercept
interviewing (CLT Facility) vas conducted in the following
twelve markets: Boston, Chicago, Dallas, Detroit,
Indianapolis, Jackson, Los Angeles, Phoenix, New Rochelle,
Washington, D. c.

Bach ad was exposed through the standard 20-ad portfolio.

all within an acceptable normative range for Locker
Room unaided and total noting. They were also on a par
for trial interest, product attribute ratings, ad
racall, main point, reasons for disinterest in trying
Kent and perceived tar level.

- There vere, however, differences for the various ads
with regard to image attributes.

Carcinogenicity

UV radiation is ketown to be @ human carcinogen, Human studies have shown that
exposure to solar radiation is causally related to skin cancer, and that use of sunlamps or simbeds
is associated with skin and eye cancer. A wide spectrum of UV radiation is carcinogenic in
experimental animals.

Solar radiation has been determined to cause cutaneous malignant melanoma and non-
melanocytic skin cancer in humans, but the relationship of solar radiation to melanoma of the eye
38 less clear (reviewed in LARC, vol. 55, 1992). Recent case-contro! studies have found strong.

‘a. sociations of cutaneous malignant melanoma with exposure to sunlamps or sunbeds (Swerdiow
et al., 1988; Walter et al. 1990; Autier et al. 1994; Westerdah! et al., 1994), especially ia
individuals under 30 and those who experience sunburn, Dose-responses for increasing duration
of exposure were observed. Malignant melanoma of the aye is also associated with use of
sunlamps (reviewed in IARC, vol. 55, 1992). There is little support for an association of
sunlamps or sunbeds with nonmelanocytic skin cancer {reviewed in IARC, vol. $5, 1992). There
is inadequate evidence in humans for the carcinogenicity of exposure to fluorescent lighting
(reviewed in IARC, vol, $5, 1992).

There is sufficient evidence for the carcinogenicity of solar radiation, broad-spectrum UV
radiation, UVA radiation, UVB radiation, and UVC radiation in experimental animals (reviewed in
IARC, vol. 55, 1992). There is clear evidence for benign and malignant skin tumors in mice, rats,
hamsters, opossums, and fish, and for tumors of the cornea and conjunctiva in rats, mice and
hamsters. Solar rediation and nonsolar UV radiation cause mutations or other genetic alterations
ina variety of im vitro and in vivo assays for genetic toxicity.

Summary, The steady-state level of the PCP mRNA was investigated
during HL-60 cell differentiation, When cells were induced to

levels were observed. In contrast, cells cultured in the presence
of the phorbol] ester TPA, which promotes the cells to be
differentiated into monocytes/macrophages, were associated with a
marked decrease in the steady-state concentration of PCP mRNA. When
cells were simultaneously treated with a combination of TPA and
staurosporine, a protein kinase ¢ (PKC) inhibitor, the TPA-elicited
decrease in PCP mRNA was partially prevented. These data euggest
that PCP mRNA expression is not directly linked to HL-60 cell
differentiation but appears to involve the participation of PKC.
Introduction.

promyelocytic leukemia, have been widely employed to investigate
the process of myeloid differentiation (1-3). HL~60 cells can be
induced te differentiate into granulocytes with dimethyleulfoxide
(DMSO) or retinoic acid (RA) (4-10), and into monocytes/macrophages
with the tumor promoter 12-0-tetradecancy} phorbel-13-acetate (TPA)
or 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, (vit D,) (7, 13-16). Proteoglycans are
macronolecules composed of one or more sulfated glycosaminoglycan
chains covalently linked to a core protein. In HL-69 cells, the
giycosaminoglycan is predominately chondroitin sulfate (17). A
number of studies analyzing ("5)-sulfate incorporation into
chondroitin sulfate in HL-60 cells have shown that treatment with
TPA, RA, or DMSO resulted in a 20-30%, 30-50%, and 45% decrease,
respectively, in sulfate incorporation (17-20). These data suggest

are measurable by state, are associated with different levels
of mortality. When two factors are correlated with a specific
mortality, #e want to sce whether each factor is still corre-
lated when correction is made for the effect of the other
factor. The correlations are across the four states,

One can say, thus, if levels of Factor A are high, mortality
iu also high, or vice veraa. Similarly, Factor B may be tested.
What must be determined is whether Factor A ia acting through
Factor @ on mortality, or Vhether it is independant and acting
en its own. For example, assume that low income levels and law
breast cancer mortality occur in the same states, also that higher
levels of breast cancer mortality and larger percentages of for
aign born occur together in the game states. Does this mean that
high incomé levels and large percentages of foreign born occur
together in the same etatea? If ao, then either income level or
ethnic background is effecting the mortality rate directly, but

jot both. If the two factors are not themselves correlated, then
ch one is effecting the mortality separately. If this isthe
case, it is aleo possible that the effact of income and the effect
of ethnic background, though independant of each other, may in~
teract on mortality go that their effect together is greater than
the sum of their individual effects. Tt could then be that being
affluent has an enhancing affect on tha ethnic influences an
mortality, or that being foreign born enhances the income level
effect on mortality. This 1s called interaction and it can be
positive or negative,

There are many ways of determining the form and extent of the
influence of several factors on the dependant variable, tha mor
tality rate, Another method besides using multiple regression
equations, is principle components, These are linear functions

of the factor variables, with coefficients which reflect the
importance and input from each factor.

When there are many interrelated factors to deal with, the
principle components reduce them to the one or two which a¢count
for most of the variation among the whole sct of factors. The
principle components are not correlated with each other, so each
one represents an independant set of forces acting upon mortality.
For example, 4f you were looking at all the body measurements as

@ linear sum function of all the measurenents with all positive
coefficients, representing a general size component, The second
Principle component would be a linear function of ail the measure-

LOW DENSITY ROD (Ti1ler)

4n Increased amount of condensed "vapor phase" has been found in TPH
collected from low density rods compared with TPM collected from a control
cigarette. This has been established by comparing the weight lost from the
appropriate TPM sample between 25 and 200°C, Lewis Haws provided condensate
collected on a standard TPM pad while Ralph Hellams provided liquid
condensate collected by the impaction trap method. A substantial amount of
material 19 "vaporized" from the low density red condensate collected on TPM
pads compared with that from a conventional 750 mg cigarette. This
difference 1s especially pronounced between 60° and 110°C. The differences
are much less evident when the impaction trap condensates are examined since
this method of collection ts much less efficient at collecting condensible
vapor phase.

One feature of the impaction trap condensate from either the control or
the low denatty rod is a sharp weight loss peak at 65°C. This is of
Interest since it 1s not present fron either TPM pad condensate sample.

EFFECTS OF HEATING RATE ON THERMAL DECOMPOSITION (Waymaek, <ang)

Several classes of compounds were pyrolyzed in a pyroprobe at nominal
heating rates of 100° and 1000°C/sec, These were long chain (¢ > 8)
alkanes, alkyl substituted benzenes, carotene and dextrose char. A
difference in partitioning between volatillzation and decomposition of the
starting material te seen as a functon of heating rate. in addition, the
distribution of aromatic ring compounds changes with heating rate.

A demonstrably significant reduction in selected condensed aromatic
fing compounds has been observed in the pyrolysis of tobacco which hae been
Supereritically extracted with G02. Further work will be undertaken to
identify what in addition to alkaloids has been extracted.

Function (Calvin et a1). Tn pulmonary artery banded dogs, right ventricular
Function 1n response to partial pulmonary artery banding remains normal, while

fatlure and systemic shock (Fixler et al). Similar studies detaiting the
changes in right ventricular contractile function in response to progressive

An enzymatic degradation step is used in the extraction of
protein from tobacco. In order to optimize this enzyme

mediated extraction of proteins from tobacco, the activity

of the enzyme (pronase) was tested using the protease

detection kit supplied by Bic RAD, ‘The method used is

based on radial diffusion of agar gel containing milk

casein. The relative concentration of protease may be e
read directly, It was possible to detect as low a pronase
activity of 0.001 units. One unit of enzyme will hydrolyze
casein to produce a colour equivalent to 1 y mole of tyrosin

per minute at 7.5 pl at 37°C.

at 0h sw

We were not able to detect an endogenous proteolytic activity
in our tobacco extract samples.

However, the whole test is carried out in a Tris buffered
physiological saline solution at 7.2 pH. By adding

tobacco extract to the protease detection kit the activity of
the pronase of the same concentration changed, which revealed
that the enzyme had not the same activity and that it was
not working up to the optimum that it could reach.

As the conditions of the test system are obviously not the
same as those of the tobacco extract we will have to use anothe
method : (3H) tritium labelled protein (a modified method of
Bolton and Hunter) (3) to follow the activity of the enzyme.

‘The aim of the project was to investigate the possibilities of
reducing the pyrolysate fraction of total smoke particulates {TPH}
of a cigarette while maintaining the TPM and nicotine (68) levels.
‘Theoretically this could be achieved by increasing the biologi-
cally inactive tar fraction by adding of “inert substances" to
cigarette filler. These inert substances should, in an ideal ca
distill undecomposed and compete with the pyrolys fraction of

tar. Thie would effect a reduction of the relative fraction of

pyrolysate in tar, and it would also decrease the absolute frac-

tion of pyrolysates in TPN, provided the TPM level is kept rm,
constant by other means.

Since humactante are biologically inactive and axe transferred un-
changed in significant amounts to the smoke particles, they,

together with volatile plaetisisers, vere used to investigate the
above-mentioned concept.

SUMMARY

‘Two fon exchange resins, the resin used in the Fact F85 filter
and Duolite A-7 a product of Diamond Shamrock Chemical Co., have
been evaluated for gas phase efficiencies and flavor properties
on a Lark type sample cigarette, 29K. The Fact resin has tenta-
tively been identified as an ion exchange resin similar to Duolite
aT.

Each resin was found to be as efficient as SCCW charcoal
in HCN removal; for organic gas phase constituents both resins
were considerably less efficient than SCCW, Fact-41%, Duolite
A7-368 as compared to 68% removal for SCCW charcoal.

The two resins and SCCW charcoal produced essentially the
same smcke pH.

Flavorwise, neither of the resing were found to be Lark
like, both exhibiting higher raw green tastes.

PROGRESS DURING OCT.-NOV., 1975

Two ion exchange resins, the resin used in Fact P85 and
Duolite A7--a product of Diamond Shamrock Chemical Co., have
been evaluated for gas phase yields and flavor properties on
gample cigarette 29K. The Fact resin has tentatively been
identified as an ion exchange resin similar to Duolite A7.

The apparent densities of the two resins were, 9.38 g/cc for
Fact and 0.37 g/cc for Duolite A7; both resins were in the free
base form,

Sample 29K was a normal production Lark type cigarette
with an empty cavity and had no glue between the plugwrap and
mouthpiece paper. ‘the tip section of the filter was removed
and known weights of the resins {to give a 62.5% fill) were
added. The tip section was then refitted.

The two resins were also evaluated in a 50-50 mixture with
SCCW charcoal to determine if these resins would be compatible
with SCCW charcoal flavorwise.

PROJECT DESCRIPTION

TITLE: Structure-Function Studies of Tubulin:The Roles of Conserved Cysteine
Residues and The Non-exchangeable GTP Site.

INTRODUCTION

Tubulin is an essential cellular protein in eukaryotes, playing a role in
chromosome and other types of cellular movement. Progress in determining the
structure of tubulin has been very slow, and in its absence, problems related to
assigning function to specific regions of the protein are best studied using a
mutagenesis approach. | have just finished a sabbatical year in the laboratory of Dr.
Kevin Farrell at the University of California, Santa Barbara, where | learned the
techniques and methodologies used to mutagenize yeast tubulin. For the most part,
my studies of tubulin and microtubules have had a chemical orientation, and | now
Pian to add this approach to my laboratory atthe University of Kaneas. wil be used 10
study the role of nucleotides in microtubule assembly and stability and to address
questions concerning the function of specific amino acid residues and regions of the
protein molecule, Abbreviated background and experimental sections are presented
below. Because of space limitations references have been omitted.

OVERALL GOALS AND SPECIFIC AIMS

The overall goal of this study is to identity amino acid residues in tubulin that are
hypothesized to be involved in GTP binding and/or hydrolysis and others that may be
involved in dimer-dimer interactions during microtubule assembly. In this proposal we
shall concentrate on the following aims.
1, To mutagenize residues in u-tubulin that may be involved in nucleotide
binding.
2. To change conserved cysteine residues to alanines.
3. To study the effects of these mutations on the biochemical properties of yeast
tubulin and on certain cellular microtubule-dependent processes.

BACKGROUND AND SIGNIFICANCE
GTP binding sites, GTPase activity, and microtubule stability.

Microtubules (MTs) are essential elements in several cellular processes,
including chromosome movement and intracellular transport. Cytoplasmic and mitotic
MTs are dynamic structures, meaning that in a population of MTs, individual MTS
efongate or shorten wie the total mass of MTs remains constar, Le. al steady state
This behavior is thought to be a necessary property as the MTs search for their targets,
4g. Kinetachores. It is also thought to be involved in the movement of chromosomes
during mitosis. This dynamic nature of MTs is undoubtedly under cellular control as
mitotic MTs grow and shorten at rates which are 20- to 100-fold higher than
cytoplasmic MTs.

The protein component of MTs is tubulin, a heterodimeric molecule with two
guanine nucleotide binding sites, an exchangeable site (E-site) on the #i-subunit and a
non-exchangeabie site (N-site) an the o-subunit. The N-site is always occupied by
GTP while the E-site is occupied by GTP or GDP. During the addition of a tubulin dimer
to the end of a growing MT, GTP al the E-site undergoes hydrolysis to GDP and Pi
One model for dimer addition to a MT proposes thal an incoming tubulin molecule
induces the hydrolysis of the GTP in the former terminal dimer. A consequence of this
model is that a MT is capped at the ends by tubulin-GTP molecules.

Intracellular free calcium in single cells can
be quantitated using the ACAS Interactive Laser
Cytometer. Small changes in free calcium can
have dramatic effects on many cellular processes
including cell motility, muscle contraction, gap
junction function, fertilization and cell division
(5).

Free intracellular calcium levels arc typically
between 50 and 150 nM, These levels ace
approximately 20,000 times lower than the total
calcium concentration estimated to be bound in
mammalian cells or in the external mitieu. Most
of the calcium within a cell is sequestered within
nuclei, mitochondria, end endoplasmic and
sarcoplasmic reticuli, Multiple mechanisms in the
cell maintain this enormous —conoentration
gradient. Other mechanisms ace responsible for
relcasing free calcium ions into the cytosol in
response to a variety of environmental stimuli
6).

Calcium ions are therefore regarded as “one
of the most important intracellular messengers in
biology" (7.8). Since the changes in free calcium
levels which trigger cell activity are minute and
often very rapid, quantitating these changes has
been technically very difficult, Recently,
however, Roger Tsien and his associates (9) have
developed a fluorescent calcium indicator, Indo-l,
which is particularly well suited for quantitating
rapid caleium flux in single cells.

The fluorescent probe, Indo-l, is used to
monitor calcium in its bound and frec states by
exciting the dye with the 351-363 nm Lines of the
argon laser, and simulianeously detecting two
emissions at 405 nm and 485 nm (9). A ratio of
these emissions is computed automatically by the
integrated software and allows quantitation of
free calcium within the cell, The ratio method
eliminates most of the problems associated with
dye leakage and concentration of fluorescent
probe within the cell, and the simultancous
detection provides for extremely accurate
quantitation of temporal and spatial calcium
changes. The laser excitation source provides a
small beam (about ! micron in diameter} which
both allows for extremely low levels of
fluorescence to be detected with minimum laser
power, and greatly reduces the amount of
phototleaching that might accur wich repeated
scanning. The X-¥ scanning stage allows for .25

Chronic Respiratory Effects of Past and Passive Sucking
Title of Research Project (do not exceed 60 spaces}

Dept. of Public Health and Preventive Medicine, School of Medicine, Lowa Linda University
Department and

This application is requesting funds to analyze existing data pertaining to past and passive
smoking and chronic respiratory symptoms from a prospective epidemiofogical cohort study of
3,914 currently non-smoking individuais in California and to develop a future proposal which
would best utilize this stil available cohort to assess the effects of more refined measures of past
and passive smoking on lung function. Although all individuals were currently not smoking during
the prospective cohort phase of the study, 1977-1987, many had smoked in the past or had
significant exposure %o passive smoking at home or at work, The California Air Resources Board
has funded @ compilation of an extensive ambient air pollution database for this cohort spanning
2 20 year period, 1967-1987, and encompassing seven specific air pollutants. Information on
indoor sources of air pollutants have also been collected. Although the California Air Resources
Board has funded extensive analyses of this data pertaining to ambient air pollutants, they are
unwilling to fund analyses of past and passive smoking, except to adjust for these variables as
covariates in the form of summary measures only. A wealth of information on history of past
smoking and of type, time of life, and duration of different aspects of passive smoking have been
collected. Analyses of these refined measures of past and passive smoking would yield much
useful information which would be highly pertinent to the purposes of the tobacco tax funding.
Assessment of combined and possibly synergistic effects with California air pollutants can be
assessed as weli as comparison of the relative magnitude of effects of passive smoking with other
air pollutants, The primary health outcomes in this data set are cumulative ten year incidence
and persistent prevalence of definite symptoms of airway obstructive disease, chronic bronchitis,
and asthma with the incident cases of asthma confirmed through examination of physician's
records.

A portion of the effort of this application would be devoted 10 developing 2 future proposal in
collaboration with ovr consultants, Analyses of existing data will provide guidance to the design
which could best exploit this still available population which could entail additional health
measures such 2s lung function testing, or/and more refined measures of exposure to passive
smoking and other indoor and outdoor pollutants on strategically chosen subsamples of the

California A3037 was signed into law by Govemior Wilson on 9/27. A3037 extends to 1/1/98 the date
by which the standard board or EPA must adopt prescribed standards for reducing employee exposure
to ETS in order to permit continuance of the smoking ban exemptions for bars, taverns, and gaming
clubs.

SMOKING RESTRICTIONS

+ On 9/30, U.S. Rep. Lewis (D-GA) introduced HR4333, which would prohibit smoking in any
transportation facility for which federal financial assistance is provided...HR4333 was referred to the
House Transportation & Infrastructure Cie.

+ On 10/1, the W.8. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia heard oral arguments in Action on.
7. The ASH lawsuit requests the Court to
order the Labor Dept’s OSHA to issue emergency rolemaking to restricVban smoking in workplaces
nationwide, Federal OSHA proposed regulations in April 1994 on indoor air quality and ETS.

+ The Mayor of Friendship Heights, MD is proposing an ordinance that would ban the use or discarding
of tobacco products in public. The ordinance is scheduled to be discussed during a public hearing on
10/15. If approved, the measure would need the conseat of the Montgomery County Council, which is
currently considering a county-wide smoking ban of its own.

+ On 10/1, the Fort Worth, TX City Council received citizen presentations from such groups as the
American Cancer Society, Texas Smokers Rights Assn, and the Cowtown Smoker Rights. The groups
presented their viewpoints on a proposed city ordinance that would ban smoking in restaurants, public
buildings and places of employment,

OUR RESPONSE ~ Now that Philip Morris has decided to stop publicly denying the health
risks and addictiveness of smoking, the question advocates need to ask is: How are they going to

Research shows 4,800 children ty their fist cigarette every day in the US. What changes will
the company make in its marketing practices now that it acknowledges the addictive qualities of
cigarettes? ‘Will Philip Morris agree to common-sense regulations to youth access to tobacco,
‘such as bebind-the-counter sales in retail stores?

Philip Mortis senior vice presideat Steven Parrish said the conspany’s legal defense strategies
‘would not change in light of their acknowledgements, In fact the whole campaign may have
‘been developed with the courtroom in mind,

Industry analyst Martin Feldman said the campaign was motivated by juror research the
company has conducted over the past year and may help to “reduce the degree of juror anger that,
isout there." Why should Philip Morris not be responsible for damages to smokers with diseases
the company now understands to be caused by smoking?

Philip Morris’ newfound understanding of the health risks and addictiveness of cigarettes also
‘won't stop their opposition to Food and Drug Administration authority to regulate tobacco.

ABSTRACT

‘The hypothesis thal environmental tobacco smoke (ETS) increases tisk for cardiovascular
disease (CVD) in nonsmokers has recently received allention because of two published
government reports. Epidemiology studies report that the relative risk for CVD
associated with actively smoking twenty cigarettes per day is 1.78. Active smoking
exposes the smoker fo approximately 16 times the ES concentration, and 100 to 300-
fold the total smoke dose, experienced by a nonsmoker. ‘These government reports
estimate the relative risk for ischemic heart discase in BTS-exposed nonsmokers at 1.30
(CALEPA) and 1.23 (SCOTH), despite the much lower smoke exposure, Platelet
aggregation is proposed as a plausible and quantitatively consistent mechanism to explain
this nonlinear dose-response anomaly. Herein, evidence is presented suggesting that this
Tow-dose hypothesis is inconsistent with the literature om the cardiovascular pathology of
active smoking, the physiology of platelets and with the results from a human expostre
chamber study.

INTRODUCTION

The development or exacerbation of cardiovascular discase (CVD) has been called the
most serious health effect allegedly associated with environmental tobacco smoke (ETS)
exposure in nonsmokers. The California Environmental Protection Agency (CALEPA)
has estimated that approximately 47,500 CVD deaths occur in the U.S. each year due to
TS exposure [I]. ‘The U.S. BPA’s estimate for U.S. lung cancer deaths associated with
ETS is 3.80012).

‘The magnitude of the epidemiological association between CVD and active cigarette
smoking is problematic for proponents of a causal association between ETS exposure and
CVD. Specifically, the relative risk (RR) of CVD in active smokers of twenty cigarettes
per day (one pack) is reportedly 1.78 [3). Recent relative risk estimates for C'VD and
ETS include 1.30, as calculated by the California Environmental Protection Agency
(CALEPA) [1], and 1.23, as determined by the Sciemific Commitee on Tobacco and
Health (SCOTH) [4] in the U.K. According to these estimates, the CVD tisk associated
\with ETS exposure is approximately one-third of the CVD risk associated with actively
smoking twenty cigarettes per day.

Rockland, Mass, -- Earlier in December, the board of health unanimously voted to table a
proposed retail regulation until 1/14. The proposal would ban self-service displays and
‘vending machines and increase licensing fees. Bob Stone requests mai) and phone calls to
the retailers 10 urge their attendance at this meeting.

Mesa, Ariz, ~ The city council voled on 12/20 10 amend the city's smoking ban. The
ordinance allows smoking in bars and restauram bar areas. Restaurams can gct a hardship
vasiance if they can prove & 15 percent loss over a four month period. Businesses can also
have a separate outdoor smoking area and hotels can allow smoking in private, rented
‘conference rooms. Opponents of the smoking ban filed sufficient signature to force either
modification of the ban or a special election on the issue. Although 5 of the 7 councit
members said they supported a special election, the council decided to amend rather than
call for an election, The city clerk said that an election was still possible, but not Likely.
‘The amended language will go into effect 30 days from passage.

Attached are the results from a series of focus groups and a
public opinion poll (including the cross tabs) on the California
advertising campaign.

While poll respondents doubted that the ads would cause people to
stop smoking, they did feel that the ads could be effective in
discouraging non-smokers from starting to smoke. Those polled
believed the ads served an “educational function,
divided on the appropriateness of using state funds.

Those polled do believe that there are superior ways to change
behavior and to allocate state funds; namely, by funding
educational programs for children and by funding direct health
care, espacially for these who have less access to such services.
This finding is apparantly in tune with our direction in
california,

The focus panels suggest that anything done in opposition to
tobacco meets with general approval. Opposition to the ads from
the panels was not on the grounds of inappropriate use of

government funds, but because focus panel subjects felt the ads
weren't strong enough. I draw your attention to page 25 of the
"Qualitative Research Report."

These documents will be a centerpiece of our industry meetings in
California this week,

used for sidestream smoke descriptive testing, has been upgraded

sequence). Since the programs are menu driven, testing

cated for the test administrator. In addition to the benefit of

side-by-side olfactometers, making possible discrimination and
just-noticeable-difference experiments. PT&D provided the tech-
nical support and funding for this project with actual progran-
ming and installation being done by Dah Dugan, an outside
Programmer.

review of our joint program to develop effective methods for
evaluating new technology products. As a result of this progran,
Extended Usage QDA Panels for Smokers and Non-emokers were devel~
oped. Benefits and possible applications were discussed and next
steps outlined. These panels represent a major accomplishment.
for the SED methods development research program.

Dear Sir:

For several years we have been exploring the relationship between
lung mechanical properties and connective tissue change in different
models of hunan pulmonary disease, As en outgrowth of these experinents,
it has become clear that more information is needed with respect to the
normal aging processes in the lung and the interrelationship between aging
‘and normal lung repair mechaniens. For many reasons it ig apparent that it
is quite important to study pulmonary connective tissue alterations and
responecs to injury in the aging hamoter lung. The natuze of this proposed
program je briefly deseribed below.

The lung similar ro al) other organs progressively accumulates
physiological decrements with time. The Framingham study of an aging hunen
population recently reported the relation among pulmonary function aging,
cigarette smoking end mortality, A striking relation was sorted hetween
forced vital capacity and mortality which appeared to be independent of
cigarette smoking or other environmental insults, In both mon and
experimental animals the aging lung reveals loss of elastic recoil and
increase in the size of respiratory air-spaces. Both of these properties
are related to changes in the connective tissue network of the lungs.

Tt ig not clear whether these changes are due just to the mechanical wear
ané tear effects of a lifetime of breathing or to alterations in the
pulmonary connective tissue synthesizing machinery which then alters the
ability of the pulmonary connective tissue to repair itself after injury.
We intend to explore these questions by comparing physiological, struc~
tural and biochemical parameters in the whole animal es well as biochemical
studies in lung orgen and cell cultures,

We plan to use hamsters in our experiments in part becouse we have
extensive experience with these snimals including the development of
emphysema and fibrotic models of diseases and in sddition because of the
animals innate resistence to pulmonary infection. A colony of appraxi-
mately 250 onimale with an average age of 1 year has been established at our
Ynatitution. Since the hamster life span is estimated to be approximately
2h-36 months, periodically animals are removed from the colony and their
lung volumes and static compliance are measured. The animals ore then
killed and the lunge are removed for histologic studies.

P&L Results: Operating income for February was $585M versus a
plan of $423M (+$162M)7 year-to-date, the results are $250M ahead

(+8604). The backlog remains exceptional -- $27MM+, Total

moves effective during the second quarter and improvements by

Converter Foil Sales: In spite of the continued rise in aluminum
ingot costs, National Aluminum and Consolidated Aluminum have
elected to hold their 1/1/88 converter foil prices through June,
1988, If TAC chose to meet this competitive situation in order

margins, Understandably, it appears that neither of these
companies will be willing to pay TAC the approximate $,065/pound
premium required. This situation creates a 200M pound/month or
$360M sales/month hole in our 1988 plan. Internal and external
avenues to utilize this capacity will be investigated.

Asccast Reroll Capacity: In the wake of our withdrawal from the
conductor strip and litho market areas, we have uncommitted
capacity. for as-cast reroll of 400M pounds/month. Several
opportunities to utilize this capacity on an ingot tolling
contract basis are being investigated. The most promising of
these is a potential situation with National Aluminum which could
combine the sale of Mill 20 and possibly the Forte slitter with a
multi-year ingot tolling contract at a premium per pound price for
conversion.

was the preferred lidding structure for all of P&G's peanut butter

Transition from the previous lidding structure, which had a TAC
coextruded sealant film, is complete with this week's 325M linear
yards production run. The structure change moves production from
the flexo press to Extruder III.

Genesis Packaging Company: Genesis is a joint venture betwaen
Alcoa and Metal Box-United Kingdom, selling barrier plastic
packaging and machinery in the United States, Representatives who
visited TAC/Packaging Manufacturing were very impressed with our
plant and people. TAC has the lidding material of choice for the

’ XPORT RING

The House Subcommittee on Tobacco and Peanuts held its third hearing
June 14 on allegations of fraud in the export credit guarantee programs
{GSH 102 and 103), which provide loans to foreign governments to make
Anerican agricultural products affordable. According to government
investigators, 31 of the 66 tobacco leaf dealers that participated in the
GSM programs in the past three years blended cheaper foreign tobacco with
the American leaf in their shipments, but fraudulently represented to the
federal government that the tobacco was 100 percent Anerican. Representa-
tive Charlie Rose (D-NC), Chairman of the Subcommittee, said that another
hearing will be held to examine the possibility of tobacco export fraud in
transactions not involving GSM financing.

May 10, 1978

Mr. A. P. Gaspar

Re: FOCUS GROUPS FOR PROJECT "I" .

Attached to this memo is the report on the focus groups for
Project "I." My interpretation of the groups and the thoughts
given in the report are in agreement, The groups were conducted
in Cincinnat£ on April 12 and 13,

Background

The focus groups were conducted to gain a qualitative reaction to
cigarette ideas directed at sccial problems connected with smoking.
The bulk of the discussion centered on four ideas:

1. A cigarette with no sidestream smoke.

2. A cigarette with 50% less sidestream smoke.

3. A cigarette with a pleasant aroma.

4. A cigarette which did not stain teeth or fingers.

Summary of Results

1, Smokers will not accept any modification in their cigarette
which would adversely affect its taste. There seems to be a
Predisposition that any changes in a cigarette tend to adversely
affect the taste - perhaps a result of experience with low tar
cigarettes.

2, Smokers do feel that sidestream (or “raw") smoke can be bother-
some to both non-smokers and smokers due to the odor and irritation.
However, they did indicate that the smoke does have some
psychological benefits (the aroma, the visual pleasure of
watching the smoke, a feeling of comfort). Smokers were very
skeptical that a cigarette like this could deliver much taste.

3. A cigarette which gave off 50% (or noticeably) less smoke seemed
to be a reasonable compromise to many of the panel members. The
psychological benefits are retained, but there is less smoke to-
collect in a house, gather on clothes, etc. However, most
members agreed that non-smokers would remain dissatisfied as
jong as a cigarette gave off any smoke.

Recently, it was discovered that small pieces of the CPN-23 Coupon Hot
Helt Adhesive used to hold coupons on packs of cigarettes may be getting into
product when these packs are sent through P.R.0. Below is a toxicological
evaluation of the potential for health effects resulting from small quantities
of this hot melt being introduced into our products.

_ This hot melt is composed of paraffin wax, polyethylene and a hydracarbon
resin.

Paraffin wax (or hard wax) is a mixture of salid hydrocarbons consisting
mainly of alkanes. 1t is white, somewhat translucent, odorless and flammable.
It ts used as a household wax and is approved as a coating for food containers
in direct contact with food (21 CFR 176.170). {t is essentially non-toxic.
Physiologically, paraffin waxes are inert. No adverse health effects are ex-
pected from the use of this compound. The major pyrolysis products would be
primarily COz, CO, H20 and short chain hydrocarbons.

Polyethylene is a polymer of very low toxicity. Polyethylene has been
appraved for direct food contact (21 CFR 175.300, 177.1520). During thermal
degradation of polyethylene at 800°C, carbon monoxide is the principle gas
evolved. At temperatures below 500%C, COp, acrolein, formaldehyde, other
aldehydes, ketones and hydrocarbons are generated. Acrolein is the only
acutely toxic compound generated. It is an irritant to the eyes and respira~
tory tract at 5 ppm and can be lethal at 10 ppm after short periods of tine.
The American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIK} has set
the threshold Timit valve (TL¥) for acrolein at 0.1 ppm.

The hydrocarbon resin (Goodyear Wingtack 95) is a synthetic polyterpene
resin. Tt has a very low order of toxicity with an LDgp (lethal dose ta S0&
of the test population) greater than 10 g/kg in rats. It is approved for use
in resinous and polymeric coatings (21 CFR 175.300), adhesives (21 CFR
175,105) and components of paper_and paperboard in contact with aqueous and
fatty foods (21 CFR 176.170). The major pyrolysis products of this resin
would include C02, CO, H20, terpenes and other short chain hydrocarbons.

Based on the small amounts of CPN-23 expected in the tobacco rod, there
should be no adverse health effects resulting from the pyrolysis of small
pieces of adhesive. Smal) quantities of acralein and CO are formed during

Retaik 12's Cigarette Package:

Due to: problems encountered with vending the current 12's soft peckage, Marketing
has requested a high priority effort be devoted to developing a package which can be
used in the three major types of vending machines. Once prototype designs have been

PF Project:

Efforts are being made to determine what packaging requirenents are needed to
automatically tax stamp a 94 nm length product in a crush proof box. Produation of
this product ds to begin in late March or early April, therefore, Marketing is most.
anxious that any tax stamping problems be resolved as quickly as possible.

Cigarette Package Inneriine

* A test was conducted on Hercules .00025 foil/opaque polypropylene innerliner for
cigarette packages. Initial testing required an air-resist manifold, made by
Hercules, which improved the feed characteristios. Laboratory tests will
continue to confirm ny benefits of the new structure.

© A test was conducted on Golden Belts printed (no foil) innerliner. This ran
well in the RAD plant. Moisture loss and odor tests are underway.

Wrepping Materials Hachinability:

Ten pallets of Miquel y Costas 719 cigarette paper were evaluated successfully at
Whitaker Park with no split ends. ‘This paper will be used as an alternate source of
supply when qualified.

1230 eigarette seam adhesive, we have instigated a new polley which requires
factories to use seam athesive in distinctive blue plastic closed head drums. The
adhesive is pumped from drums directly into the seam adhesive tank, thus reducing
the chanee for adhesive mismatch. Use of seam adhesive other than the 1230 seam

Subject: Methyl Bromide Dog Study Protocol - Date: March 7, 1994
Comments on the Proposed Statistical
Approach

To: Dr, Robert L. Suber From: Joel A. Seckar

1 requested that Mike Morton review the proposed statistical approach Pharmaco-LSR
plans to use for the 12-month study of methyl bromide fumigated feed in the dog. His
responses led to the following comments for Pharmaco-LSR's statisticians:

+ Since tests on the variance are not very powerful for small sample sizes, we would
recommend running Bartletts test at the .05 level rather than the .01 level, The
protocol states that Bartlett's testis being run as a two-sided test, We assume that this
is a misstatement. We presume that Pharmaco-LSR will not reject the null hypothesis
if the Bartlet test statistic is too small!

+ Ifthe variances are unequal (significant Bartlet’s test), itis true that the ANOVA
assumptions are violated, but s0 are the assumptions of the Kruskal-Wallis test (it also
assumes a common vatiance, though it allows for a non-normal error distribution; for
instance, see p. 115 of Hollander and Wolfe). If Bartlett's test shows unequal
variances, we would recommend transforming the data rather than using the non-
parametric set of tests. Presumably either a square root or a logarithmic
transformation would mitigate the heterogeneity of variance,

«In examining the data for trend, we would test the data using a linear contrast with the
anova error term. This is equivalent to testing the mean square from the regression
against the pure error term that is used to test for lack of fit, The straight regression
includes the lack of fit 1erm in addition to the pure error term as part of the error---if
there is curvature, this will inflate the error term, Ifitis desired to test if there is
curvature, a test of the quadcatic term vs. the pure error term can be run (or, for that
matter, the standard lack of fit test can be run).

I recommend that Pharmaco-LSR be instructed to speak directly to Mike Morton at 910-
741-4809 on any questions or comments that evolve from the above comments.

Subject: Evaluation of Aluminum Oxide Date: June 17, 1987
as a Cigarette Paper Ingredient

To: Dr, Tom Perfetti From: Dr. Scott Appleton

I have reviewed the data you provided me regarding aluminum oxide as a
cigarette paper ingredient. These data include amounts of aluminum, silicone
and magnesium in cigarette ash and smoke condensate from cigarettes with a
wrapper containing 11% and 22% aluminum oxide, In addition, fiber morphology
data on aluminum oxide and cigarette paper containing 11% and 22% aluminum
oxide were evaluated.

The fiber morphology data indicated that most of the fibers were Tess
than 2 um in length. flo asbestos fibers were identified in the samples an-
alyzed. One of the samples contained 2 fibers with lengths of about 3.5 um of
ja’ fibers analyzed. One of these fibers was very thins its width was 0.05 um
and aspect ratio was 77. The other was retatively wide; its width was 0.91 um
and aspect ratio was 4,0.

According to the data in T. R. White’s February 27 and March 2 memos to
you, addition of aluminum oxide to cigarette paper increased the smoke conden-
Sate aluminum and silicone levels by approximately 4.4 and 22.4 fold respec-
tively compared to a WINSTON control cigarette. Smoke condensate content of

increase in ash aluminum far exceeds the amount of aluminum oxide original ly

from 0.67% in contro] WINSTON ash to 4.37% in ash from cigarettes containing
aluminum oxide. These data suggest that the majority of the aluminum oxide
remains in the cigarette ash.

human health hazard. Studies indicate that combined exposure of aluminum
‘oxide and cigarette smoke could alter the biological activity of smoke compo-
nents. (For more details see attached memo.) Tt would be desirable therefore
ff none of the aluminum oxide added to cigarette paper transferred into smoke.

From the available data it is not possible to conclude with confidence
whether or not aluminum oxide transfers into mainstream smoke, The presence
of aluminum oxide in cigarette paper increased CSC aluminum by over 4-Fold.

relatively small however and there is no way of knowing whether it {s due to
transfer of aluminum oxide into smoke, altered disposition of endogencus leaf
aluminum due to altered burn rate of the tobacco rod, sample contamination or

lubricants and acrylic resins. The monomers have a low to moderate order of

and edema. The American Conference cf Governmental Industrial Hygienists
{ACGIH) has set threshold limit values (TL¥’s) for acrylates in the range of
10-25 ppm. Polymer{zed acrylates have a very low order of toxicity and are

TO: Mr. G. W, McKenna
RE: PACKAGING DEVELOPMENT DIRECTION

This provides the Brand's recommended direction for the next
phase of packaging development work for VANTAGE. With your
approval, we will direct Mort hibby and May Bender accordingly.

As we discussed, the Brand Group believes that the opportunity
to make @ compelling and preemptive statement for VANTAGE should
focus on the Brand's user image benefits. We are convinced that
any product statement that we could make through packaging would
be eithe 1) not preemptive, or 2) not believable to the
target smoker. We do, however, have an opportunity to make a
statement about the prospective VANTAGE user which uniquely
addresses the image wants of 25-34 year-old smokers who are
switching from their initial brand choice, typically Marlboro.

These switchers can be described as smokers who are putting
their adolescence behind them and are beginning to reach for the
symbols and goals which characterize younger adulthood,
Berformance to them means working hard for the goal and the
excitement of seeing it pay off, not only for today, but for
their futures. More importantly, these people are not just
achievers, but doers. Success doesn't necessarily come easily
to them but rather requires an intensity of effort, and they
have the maturity and self-confidence that it takes to make it.
A prime illustration would be the comparison of Larry Bird, who
has developed the skills of a top-notch athlete through hard
work and effoft, versus a Dr. J type; a consummate achiever, but
one to whom success comes rather easily.

VANTAGE packaging should make, a statement about the VANTAGE
smoker which exemplifies his/her new approach to life. In the
same manner that a Marlboro pack says "one of the crowd,” or
Players says “young, urban professional,” the VANTAGE pack
should say “doer/mover.“ It should possess a vibrancy and sense
of “energy ahd even an attitide of assertiveness which charac-
terizes the prospective smokers’ mindset.
