Subject: Meeting of the Committee on Nicotine Analogue Research

On Wednesday, November 26, the first meeting of the Committee on
Nicotine Analogue Research ({CONAR) was held. The purpose of the
committee, as we understand it, is toe organize an ex-house research
program to investigate known nicotine analogues, or to identify new
ones, that have minimal cardiovascular activity.

The time frame for organizing this program, by request of executive

management, would be first quarter, 1987. The following personnel have,

agreed to serve on the committee: Or. P.M. Lippielle, Dr. Thomas’
Perfetti, Dr. d. Den deBethizy, Dr. Stephen Sears, Pr. John Robinson,
Dr. Michael Dube, Or. Sam Simmons and Dr. John Reynolds (ex officio).

During the course of the meeting it was decided that the mest
reasonable appreach would be to organize the program around basic
research efforts, as opposed to treating a dedicated in vivo / in vitro
screening and testing program for synthetic analogues. This is because
the identification of unequivecal pharmacological and cardiovascular
endpoints is limited by the state-of-the-art knowledge of nicctine’s
effects in general. The consensus was that the final program should be
based on exploring the effects of analogues as part of a more general
effort to further define and understand the effects of nicotine.
Therefore it is desirable to make use of laboratories already funded by
RJR that are in a position te test analogues (e.g. Collins, Deadwyler,
Crooks, Vessell, etc.). At least one of these laboratories has synthetic
Capabilities as well. Tt was felt that this approach would facilitate
starting the program and minimize the time and expense asseciated with
the organization of mew and independent projects in, additional
laboratories.

It was also decided that the program would most easily be structured
as two separate, but integrated parts. One would involve the procurement
and/or synthesis of appropriate analogues; this should be supported by a
sound stucture-activity (SAR) research effort. The other would be based
on suitable testing paradigms tor the compounds obtained, relying on the
best pharmacological and cardiovascular endpoints available. The major
task of the committes, at present, is to identify both the endpoints and
laboratories that support research in the appropriate areas.

At present, the committee is gathering information Dr. Perfetti
Will research the identity and availability of existing nicotine
analogues. Dr, deBethizy will identify suitable endpoints to study
cardiovascular effects. Drs. Lippiello and Robinson will identify both
in vitro and in vivo pharmacological endpoints, focusing primarily on
ENS effects. Drs. Dube and Sears will explore the available ex-house
expertise in analogue synthesis and evaluation of compounds using
computerized SAR technology. Or. Simmons will serve as 4 consultant to
the committee in support of all of these areas.

well, rather than doing @ tittle in a lot of diverse areas.

Numerous other technologies will be addressed in some of our ongoing
programs and support programs. However, the following is where the primary
emphasis will be.

FLAVOR TECHNOLOGY

require special processing. However, there may also be opportunity to find
unique tobaccos that offer distinct product advantages.

and Ammoniated-Cased KCPS

To: Millie Wong From: F. Aubrey Thacker, Jr.

This essential oi1 study (EOS) of KCPS has been completed and a table
Of results is attached. Most EOS components were identified by GC/MS while
a few were identified solely by gas chromatographic retention times.

A. Cased vs Untreated" KCPS

Compared with untreated KCPS, cased KCPS essential oi1 has increased
amounts of carbohydrate degradation products, neophytadiene, 2-acetylpyrrole,
4-vinylphenol, guaiacol, indole, myristic and palmitic acids. These increases
may be explained by heat degradation of certain unknown tobacco compounds
during drying of cased KCPS.

Compounds lowered by casing are pyrrole, 2- and 3-methylbutyric acids,
hexanofc acid, 3-acetyipyridine, s-nicotyrine, 2,3'-bipyridine, &q, Kg, benzyl
alcohol, and 2-phenylethanol. Decreases of these essential of} components are
due to a net reduction in tobacco weight per sample and losses of volatile
compounds caused by the drying cycle after the casing operation.

B._ Ammoniater

sed vs Cased KCPS

Methyl pyrazine and 2,6-dimethyl pyrazine are found in the essential of] of
the ammoniated product. These pyrazines are formed by the reaction of anmonia
with sugars present in the cased KCPS. Other EOS components present in larger
amounts for the anmoniated-cased KCPS are 2- and 3-methylbutyric acids, hexanoic
acid, neophytadiene, pyrrole-2-carboxyaldehyde, Kg, Kp, 4-vinylphenol,
2,3'-bipyridine, and -nicotyrine. Compounds found in decreased amounts are
carbohydrate degradation products, 3-isopropyl-s-valerolactone, solanone,
oxysolanone, 4-hydroxydamascone, vanillin, 4-keto-a-ionol, myristfc, penta-
decanoic, and palmitic acids. These resuits are consistent with and might
have been anticipated from previous E0S data on amuoniated tobacco.

C. Anmoniated-Cased vs Untreated KCPS

Methylpyrazine and 2,6-dimethylpyrazine are present in the essential off
of the ammoniated KCPS. Gther EOS components found at higher levels in the
treated sample are carbohydrate degradation products, neophytadiene, 2-acety!-
pyrrole, 4-vinylphenol, indole, myristic and palmitic acids. Apparentty
ammoniation is not able to compensate fully for the rise in carbohydrate
degradation products caused by casing. Lower amounts of solanone, benzyl
alcohol, 2-phenylethanol, oxysolanone, g-nicotyrine, 4-hydroxydamascone,
vanillin and 4-keto-a~iono? are found in ammonfated-cased KCPS relative to
untreated product.

SUBJECT: Use of Asparagine in Cigarette Products

We recently acquired information that Philip Morris has been purchasing
significant quantities of asparagine (possibly up to 30 metric tons per
year) for at least the last two or three years. Our conjecture is that
it is being used for reaction flavor development. A literature search
identified a Philip Morris patent, U.S. 4,379,464, 1983, describing the
reaction of reducing sugars with ammonium hydroxide or ammonium salts
in the presence of trace amounts of amino acids, including asparagine,
to produce reaction flavors for use in smoking products. A separate
reference, Koehler and Odell, J. Agr. Food Chem. 18, No. 5, 895,
(1970), describes the production of alkylpyrazine compounds from sugar~
amine acid reactions, Greatest yields were attained utilizing
asparagine as the amino acid.

The sugar-amine acid reaction occurs at temperatures above 100 degrees
Celcius. This indicates three immediately apparent possibilities: (1)
The reaction flavors are produced in vitro and applied to tobacco
material; (2) The reaction constituents are placed on tobacco material
and dryers utilized to drive the reaction; and/or (3) The reaction
constituents are applied to tobacco material and the heat of combustion
during smoking utilized to drive the reaction.

Competitive Brands reports that RJR's exoding competitive position
versus Philip Morris is expected to worsen over the next three years
according to the most recent Operating Plan Update. RJR's inability to
penetrate the young adult smoker market and its disadvantage in the
lights/low tar category are keys to this decline. Neither critical
area can be totally explained by RIR advertising or marketing
strategies, nor can these areas be explained by routine analytical
testing which has not been helpful in identifying meaningful
differences between RJR and PM products. Therefore, product
differences which current methods cannot identify must account for some
of this dilemma.

Based on the above information, APT, Competitive Brands, and S. Simmons
propose preliminary research which may identify flavor differences that
account for some of our competitive disadvantages and form a basis for
development work for improved performance in the described critical
areas. We plan to begin the investigation through the following
analyses of PM products and RIR products:

PRODUCTION

Clary Sage Products (Clary Sage Oil, Sclareol, Sclareolide)

The harvesting of Clary Sage and the production of Clary sage
Oil and Sclareol began on June 6. Harvesting was complete on
June 12, The early start date and short harvest period were due
to the severely reduced quantity of plant material in the field.
The Clary Sage was severely damaged by a drought condition in March,
April and May. The yield of Clary Sage Oil was 2085 pounds versus
6000 budgeted and the yield of Sclareol was 6466 pounds versus
12,800 budgeted. It is anticipated that inventories of Clary Sage
Oil and Sciareol (held as insurance against such a disastrous harvest)
will be sufficient to meet primary customer contracts,

Sclareolide production was interrupted for Clary Sage processing.
Year to date, a total of 7668 pounds have been produced or 76%
of budget.

Wheat

Harvesting of winter wheat is now complete. Yields were 508
below normal due to severe drought to April and May.

Pobacce

The tobacco crop appears exceptionally good having been under
irrigation. Topping and suckering are on schedule.

Peanuts

The planting of peanuts was delayed due to dry weather in
May. Stands are acceptable and herbicide and land plester applica-
tions are on schedule.

Soybeans
Fall soybeans have been planted and have germinated.
Watermelons

Watexmelons are in their rapid growth stage at this time.
The melons have a fair fruit set.

SUBJECT. Tragon Test: Products Designated "Product 1" to "Product 12" in Memo of 3/14/89.

PROPOSAL NO, A8905

The Human Research Review Committee met on March 14, 1989 to consider your proposal. The
consensus of the Committee is that your proposal is approved, subject to the following limitations:

1. Clarification of the formulae of the following materials must be provided to Scientific Affairs
before the products are tested:

H&R WM flavor, RIR # 185, IFF CPP, RIR # 192, AGP-9880, AGP-986, AGP-979, AGP-991,
AGP-992,

2. In the future, products containing tobacco extracts produced by any method must be discussed
with Scientific Affairs before approval will be granted. Specifically, the evaluation of the potential
for urethane formation in various extraction processes, or in different tobacco types subjected to a
given process, must be determined, Nicotine concentrations in extracts should also be reported.

3. Results of Ames Tests on aerosols and flavors must be completed and satisfactorily reviewed with
Dr. JR. Hayes, before products can be tested

Ifyou have questions or comments, please refer to the Proposal Number above in communications
regarding this proposal

Subject: Proposed Formation of a GC-MS Spectroscopy _ Date: July 15, 1975
Working Group

To: Dr, Anders H. Laurene From: Dr. C. R. Green
Dr. Alan Rodgnan

Memorandum:

For at least the last five years the Analytical Division has provided
mass spectral analysis as a service to the Chemical Division. Since the
usage of glass capillary gas chromatography has become more common the
chromatographers have found that they must work more and more closely with
the mass spectroscopist, In fact, the mass spectrometer has become the detector
of choice for glass capillary gas chromatography. These two areas have become
so closely allied that the quality of the chromatographic results are often
limited by the mass spectral data and conversely the quality of the mass data
is often limited by the efficiency of the chromatographic columns. For these
reasons 1 propose the formation of a GC-HS spectroscopy working group consisting
of Fred Thome, George Young and myself.

The long-range goals of this group would be as follow:

1, Continue research into the preparation of efficient, stable
and inert glass capillary columns.

2, Continue research into techniques for the best utilization
of glass capillary columns such as "splitiess injection" and
“closed loop" headspace sampling {not Tenax).

3. Prepare glass capillary columns for al] members of the Research
Dept. and give advice for their proper and full utilization.

4, Prepare glass capillary columns especially for use with the
mass spectrometer.

5. Develop improved techniques for interfacing glass capillary
columis te the mass spectrometer.

6, Develop the techniques of combined GC-Specific on mass
spectroscopy.

7. Develop the techniques of combined GC-Chemica? Ionization
mass spectroscopy.
